Hall, from Hall to Augusta Foss Heindel, from her to her husband Max Heindel, and from Heindel to one of his teachers, Rudolf Steiner. Steiner was the wunderkind of early twentieth century central European occultism, a brilliant Austrian philosopher who chucked an academic career to teach occultism. He was active in the Theosophical Society until Annie Besant's misbehavior got intolerable, and then quit with most of the other central European members to found a new organization, the Anthroposophical Society. Heindel studied with him before the split, when Steiner was exploring a more traditional occultism using rituals from the Masonic rite of Memphis and Misraim, and much of Heindel's work draws on Steiner's earlier teachings -- as well as the work of another man, a teacher they had in common, whom we'll discuss next week.

Jeff

replies.title: Re: Completed First Grade of Dolmen Arch and Lack of Rituals for Grades in DA
replies.content:
1) Delighted to hear it.

2) Occult correspondence courses in the years between the two world wars rarely had any kind of ritual to mark the division between grades. The original Dolmen Arch course probably didn't have any rituals at all -- the material as I received it was too fragmentary to be sure, but most such courses didn't; I decided, after reflection, that the SoP and certain other ritual practices were worth adding to it, but that's one of the few places where I departed from standard early 20th century practice. I figured adding initiation rituals would be too big of a divergence.

comments.title: The Free Masons
comments.content: So I have always wanted to join the free Mason's and recently I have gotten an opportunities to do so. However recently through conversations with a Mason, he didn't seem like he had any occult knowledge, he seemed like he was a materialistic beliefs . I always thought the Mason's had some very clear occult tradions so. Are the Mason's an occult order? Do they stay away from the occult now a days so modern masons people don't think deeper then standard faternaty ritual (not to knock fraternity rituals)? Will join the masons help me in m yoccult past?

replies.title: Re: The Free Masons
replies.content: That's suppose to say occult path* not past

replies.title: Re: The Free Masons
replies.content: No, Freemasonry isn't an occult order. It has a very rich tradition of ritual and symbolism, but it doesn't teach occultism, and any man who believes in a Supreme Being can join the Masons -- meaning that it gets a lot of members who are ordinary church-going Christians and also a lot of members who have very little interest in spirituality but agree that there's probably a Supreme Being. That said, a lot of occultists have become Masons over the years -- I'm one of them -- partly because of that very rich tradition of ritual and symbolism and partly because so many occultists have joined it in the past, and borrowed elements of its ritual and symbolism for occult purposes. There are also some interesting connections between 18th-century Masonry and occult traditions, but those take a lot of digging to find.

comments.title: Magic at Sea
comments.content: I’m going to be on a ship (aircraft carrier actually) for some amount of time starting next year.  What are the magical effects of being at sea vs. on land?   I’m doing  LBRP, kabbalic cross, some meditation and divination. Frankly I’m a rank beginner. I’m planning on doing ritual work “in the astral” to avoid alarming people (tight quarters, zero privacy). Anything else I should know about the sea and magic?

replies.title: Re: Magic at Sea
replies.content: I know several people who've kept up magical practices while on shipboard in the Navy. They haven't reported any particular difference, so you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: Magic at Sea
replies.content:
Hello there,

I’ve been in the US Navy for about 22 years. (I’m assuming odds are you are also US because you are going to a carrier). I’ve been an occultist for about ten of those years. I’ve been underway many times while keeping up a basic magic practice. One thing you can do especially on a carrier is ask the chaplain or the RPs if there is a time that you can use the chapel for a private practice. I’ve done this and it works great but you have to work with when it available. I made friends with the RP LPO on one deployment and he let me use a fan room off his space anytime I wanted privately. I’ve also gone the astral route as well. As far as practice I had good results, but I would avoid doing anything more complex than a basic LBRP/MP in case the ship goes to GQ or some other common ship happening, leave the big stuff for a port call. Also, I used to do divination every day easily in my rack- just shut the curtains and face the other way. Hope that helps!

replies.title: Re: Magic at Sea
replies.content: Also, one thing left out. I know it can be a concern letting people in your command know that you are an occultist. I personally do not. When I ask to use facilities aboard ship, I tell them that I have a meditation practice that I chant and move around a little bit and would like some privacy to do so for 20 to 30 minutes. That works nicely. I actually got asked by the head chaplain on my last deployment to teach a class on meditation when she saw how consistent I was with my practice. No one had any clue I was actually doing occult ritual with that meditation!

replies.title: Re: Magic at Sea
replies.content: Thanks very much for your advice, and also thanks to JMG.  I hope I’m as consistent with the practice as you were.  Cheers

comments.title: When is a tarot card reversed?
comments.content:
Since the start of this year I'm having great fun with 3 card tarot readings. I use these questions for each card: "What you think it is", "Why you don't see what it is", and "What it really is". I started with just aces, 2s and 3s and slowly including more cards. I'm now using up until the King (lesson 6 from Mastering the Tarot.) I've noted that each card has a separate explanation for "Reversed". For example, Swords VIII means "The seeker is fearful" and reversed "Relaxation from fear". So far I've ignored the reversed explanations.

When should one read a card in its reversed meaning?

replies.title: Re: When is a tarot card reversed?
replies.content:
When it's upside down in the reading. People who use reversals shuffle in a way that makes some of the cards upside down at random -- the usual trick is to rotate half the deck right after you cut it, and before you shuffle it back together. 1) I'd recommend talking to an alternative health practitioner -- an acupuncturist, an Ayurveda practitioner, a homeopath, etc. -- if those are legally available in your area. Blockages of this sort are something they're good at treating. MDs generally don't have a clue about such things.

2) Bishop Hall's book isn't something I'd recommend for a beginner! My chapter on discursive meditation in The Druidry Handbook might be better suited to that. I don't follow his specific system, but I rarely have trouble finding trains of thought related to the theme. When I draw a blank, I use a system called the sevenfold wheel of thought which is covered in The Dolmen Arch.

His system, however, might be translated as follows:

1. What is the theme? Be exact.
2. What are its parts?
3. What causes it?
4. What does it cause?
5. What is it part of?
6. What are its qualities and what things are connected to it?
7. What things are most different from it?
8. What things are most similar to it?
9. What words describe it, and what do they literally mean?
10. What sacred or philosophical texts talk about it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A few other things I sometimes use:
- If the theme is part of a narrative (like a myth), what happens in that story? Who are the other characters? What other stories are they a part of? What relationships do they have? Does this theme support, go against, or complement any of the overall points/themes/messages of the whole source?
- One the commentariat here suggested to me: after considering the literal meaning of the words used in the theme, think about synonyms or near-synonyms and ask yourself if your understanding changes when using those words instead
- Also from JMG and the commentariat: with philosophical or abstract themes, try to think of things from your experience or everyday life that at least partially demonstrate the concept, then try to think of some others
- If there are symbols (either pictorial or described), consider what you know about the literal thing used as a symbol - for example, if there's a raven involved, think about the actual large carrion-eating corvids and what you know about them

Hope these help, and good luck!
Jeff

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
Greetings dear JMG and everyone here,

1) I want to learn more about Atlantis and hyperborea. I am dimly aware that you've mentioned atleast Atlantis as a real place, but I am keen to learn more about its history, how advanced it was and its relationship to today. Could you point me to material or share your views here?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) Nobody knows. Seriously, the civilization that's dimly recalled by the Atlantis legend was drowned beneath rising sea levels more than 10,000 years ago and very, very little in the way of information remains. Charles Hapgood's book Maps of the Ancient Sea Kings is worth reading, as it covers one of the few really solid sources of data; my own book on Atantis, now out of print, was badly flawed and I don't recommend it. (I'll be writing another book on the subject eventually.) As for Hyperborea, we know even less about that, since it was in the early part of the last ice age. I've come to think that all four of the ancient cycles of civilization discussed in occult literature -- the Polarian, Hyperboreean, Lemurian, and Atlantean cycles -- were very likely historical realities, but the sheer length of time that separates us from those cycles makes it very difficult for us to know much. (In the same way, once our cycle ends, our civilizations will be remembered as dim myths from before the beginning of the history of some future age.)

2) As for the Kali Yuga, there are plenty of traditions from around the world that suggest that we're in a relatively more materialistic and difficult period than was true many millennia ago, and that seems reasonable to me. Since this is where I am, I figure that there's good reason for that -- but any kind of detailed response to your questions would require an entire book, not a brief response in a journal post!

comments.title: Converting tables
comments.content: So Table 3 of the Ckicken Qabalah is entitled The Higharchy of Spirits. If I were to attempt to convert the table to some other pantheon, say the celtic one I think converting the divine names would be as simple as inserting the gods in who have domain over the corresponding spheres on the tree of life (ie. Byw spheres divine name would be Elen) but how would I go about finding good substatutes of the Archangels the angels the intelligences and the spirits?

replies.title: Re: Converting tables
replies.content: This is known in some magical circles as the Plug-and-Play Fallacy. No, you can't just swap out one set of names with another. Some pantheons fit the Tree of Life more or less, but others don't, and even if the gods fit, there are generally major differences in worldview that affect the other levels. This is a good example -- the Jewish Cabala has linear hierarchies of spiritual beings, divine name to archangel to angel to intelligence to spirit, but Celtic culture doesn't work that way; it's much less linear, and so instead of a nice neat line you've got a tangled gallimaufry of entities who can't be assigned clear connections to one another. Sorry!

In New England in the 1600s the Native American specialists in magic were called "pawaws" by the English colonists. So what a pawaw did was his "pawawing" (plural, "pawawings," obviously).

As for the Indian tribe in question, there was a few tribes that would have an interest in casting some magic against Bacon and his forces. Also, there were other tribes involved during this time, outside of the Powhatan confederation, from the Potomac River (Doegs & Susquehannocks) all the way down to the Roanoke River on the border with North Carolina (Occaneechees).

comments.title: The Second Religiosity
comments.content:
1. In a post from last week, you referred to the "Second Religiosity" (which to me sounds as though it might have some type of parallel with the "Second Death" but I am not sure). Could you explain this concept?

2. I just bought your book "Monsters" and read the chapter about demons. In there, you say that demons exist on a level of reality not accounted for in most metaphysical systems, if I understood you correctly. I inferred this to mean that it is a layer that is "denser" that the physical plane our consciousness is centered on. What is this layer called and what is known about it? (Excellent book, by the way).

replies.title: Re: The Second Religiosity
replies.content:
1) It's a concept from the historian Oswald Spengler. Every civilization has its own Age of Reason, complete with the usual skepticism, rationalism, and materialism, and every Age of Reason eventually fails because human reason isn't anything like as effective as its true believers like to think. As the Age of Reason winds down, a lot of people in the civilization go back to their traditional religion as a refuge from mental and social chaos; that's the Second Religiosity. The current revival of traditional Christianity is a good example of the type.

2) It has various names; the one that's found in Hebrew demonological writings is Gehenna, the Kingdom of Shells. It consists of the fragments of a universe that existed before our universe, and the demons are those souls incarnated in that universe who failed totally in the work of spiritual evolution and were left behind in the rubble when their universe went out of existence.

replies.title: Re: The Second Religiosity
replies.content: 2) What does a soul have to do to fail so badly?

replies.title: Re: The Second Religiosity
replies.content: Make every mistake a soul can make and just keep on making them even after realizing that they're mistakes and grasping what the consequences will be. It's usually a side effect of extreme spiritual pride -- the Christian myth of Lucifer got that right.

comments.title: Why is my skeleton trying to escape?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I am fairly new to most of the things discussed in this space, and recently decided to take up discursive meditation, following the directions outlined on this blog. I’ve tried “mindfulness”/breath meditation in the past without much success, but find that I am really enjoying discursive meditation and actually want to try to keep up with it as a daily practice (with more or less success…) unlike other kinds of meditation I’ve tried so far.

Perhaps this is my ADHD manifesting itself, or a normal (?) part of developing a practice like this, but recently, when I sit down to meditate, I have an intense tingling/tickling/crawling sensation under my skin, particularly my hands and feet. It makes me want to get up and move, stretch, and vigorously flex and scratch at my palms and the soles of my feet, which is, uh, not conducive to sitting still and contemplating anything. (To be honest, just thinking about meditation/writing this out is causing the sensation to flare up.)

Most times I can tolerate it and just try to push through, but there was a time recently when I just felt there was no way I could possibly endure, and I let out what I suppose could be described as a spontaneous mental cry to the Archangel Michael for help. (I am not a Christian and to my memory I have never done this before — but perhaps a combination of warm childhood associations and a recent conversation brought him to mind as a being who might be able to assist…) Afterwards, I had a brief sensation of being surrounded by a golden light and a vague perception of wings ? The tingling/crawling sensation faded to the point of being tolerable, and I proceeded with the meditation.

I currently don’t have any sort of divination or banishing rituals that I know or practice, although around the time I started trying to learn discursive meditation, I also made myself a red bag amulet as described in the FAQ. (Currently on my third one, replacing about once a month.) As a recovering materialist, I guess I’m just trying to make sense of all of this. I’m not sure what to do with the experience of randomly (?) asking an archangel to help me meditate, nor the apparent (?) response. And I’m really not sure what to make of the feeling that my muscles are trying to claw their way out of my skin whenever I try to sit still and focus on a theme. Is this a normal thing that happens? Is it a sign I should switch something up, or a test to see if I will push through? Any insights you could share would be greatly appreciated.

Much gratitude to you for hosting this space, and your patience with longwinded n00bs.
-

replies.title: Re: Why is my skeleton trying to escape?
replies.content: It's quite common for the subconscious to object to meditation practice, and to do it by way of annoying physical sensations. Calling on a helpful spiritual being is one very standard way to help deal with that if it gets too difficult on your own. Keep on pushing through, and invoke the archangel when you feel that's suitable, and you'll be fine.

replies.title: Re: Why is my skeleton trying to escape?
replies.content:
Thank you for this. I had hoped it was just "part of the process" but also wanted to check, just in case...

Two follow ups, if I might:

1) Will the weird sensations eventually go away? Or is this a thing where I'll just eventually learn to tune it out, but it'll still be there?

2) Is there a good way to express thanks/gratitude to the archangel for the assistance I receive? (I don't have a whole lot of experience with spontaneous prayer or anything like that...)

replies.title: Re: Why is my skeleton trying to escape?
replies.content:
1) Yes. Once your subconscious gets used to meditation, it'll stop kicking up a fuss.

2) Just say thank you, and direct some gratitude his way.

replies.title: Dedication
replies.content:
I believe the early Romans had similar practices of dedicating the enemy soldiers to an underworld god just before a battle. What exactly is going on here? I've been trying to understand this Roman tradition with little success for a while. I brought it up on Sane Polytheism a while back (sanepolytheism.dreamwidth.org/3248.html?thread=56240#cmt56240) but I'm still not sure if the (early) Romans, a people who were so in-your-face about honour, would throw the equivalent of a death curse as a part of a state-sanctioned rite. I keep thinking they must be doing something else, although the parallels to the lead curse tablets asking underworld gods to curse an annoying neighbor have crossed my mind. My best guess is they're making some sort of trade, like "You're a death god, I'll give you more death since these guys were going to die anyway, but now they'll die as part of the divine order, and in return you give me victory in battle." I'm still not sure though.

Is it ever appropriate to "dedicate" enemy soldiers to underworld deities? If so, what exactly is going on?

replies.title: Re: Dedication
replies.content: It's very much the equivalent of a lead curse tablet, and I don't recommend doing anything of the kind.

replies.title: Re: Dedication
replies.content:
I'm not as familiar with the Roman practice mentioned in the linked post, but I can explain a bit more what Krasskova is referencing:

In the Voluspa, Odin throws a spear over the assembled Vanir at the beginning of the Aesir-Vanir war and said "Odin Owns you All". I've encountered a lot of references to this behavior being done before real battles, with the idea being that slain foes were suitable sacrifices for a God of battle, but I'm having trouble tracking down a source, and it might be something folks infer from the Aesir-Vanir myth. We do have accounts (such as from Adam of Bremen, I think) that humans were sometimes sacrificed to Odin, but they were usually slaves or war captives. The spear-over-the-battlefield thing is usually interpreted as you say as something like "instead of just killing these guys, we're doing it as an act of devotion to you, so maybe help us out more than them."

All of which is to say she's not pulling this idea out of thin air, but given what I've learned from this community, it gives me the heebie-jeebies and further complicates my already-challenged take on how to think about Krasskova and her works. If nothing else, even if we set aside the idea that "human sacrifice is always a bad idea" (which I happen to think is true!), there's a very big difference between "offering" the people you are personally going to fight hand-to-hand, who have a fair chance to kill you first, versus paying some money for a weapon other people taking the risks will use. I don't think even the darker, scarier sides of Odin would be very pleased by that.

In other words, yikes whatever way you come at it.
Jeff

comments.title: 2023 - 1st Quarter Spread for the UK
comments.content:
Trajectory of UK for First 3 months of 2023 -

Some definitions:
Purushartha - karma performed of our own free will and volition
Sanchita - One's cosmic warehouse of karma. Typically only a tiny sliver becomes active upon each rebirth.
Prarabdha - the sliver of Sanchita karma that becomes activated upon rebirth.
Agama - Purushartha + Prarabda becomes Agama karma. Agama are karmas one plans or desires but do not get fulfilled.
Dridha - fixed/immutable/very difficult to sidestep
Adridha - fluid/mutable/easy to sidestep

1. Jan: Knight of Wands - Sri Arya says this is a peak Karma card that puts in motion yet more Karmas that will enexorably need to be fulfilled someday (ie the consequences will be exceedingly difficult to sidestep). Kinetic Karma is at its peak with this card. Whatever actions the UK takes (or fails to take) in this month will have an outsized effect for years to come. A lot of UK's (as a country) karma will get burned off this month but oddly a lot of new karma - probably very different in nature - will get created in it's place. The key is the prana of shamana - in order for something to emit light, work and heat something else has to burn. That is what this card is indicating. I'm guessing a lot of things about the UK are going to see their final moments here since this is a card of burning off a lot of karma. Or it could just mean the UK will announce some deal for getting energy bills down even further or something like that. I just don't know what. All I know is that this is the card of burning things in order to create. In fact, Sri Arya says this exact card is (his words) "Purushartha par excellence" (all you UK mages may want to meditate on that? Maybe if you want to manifest something that will have a lasting effect - well, January might be a good time to go for it?) Anyway, what gets created and whether it's of any long lasting value or improves the overall well-being of the people...I don't know that yet.

The Karma Quality is Dridha - fixed/immutable/immovable.

Suit Tattva: Fire (agni) Card Tattva: Fire of Fire (Agni of Agni) - actions that are creative and feed on creativity will be out and about.

Spiritually it's a very high card. It moves to accomplish goals at rapid speed. It also heralds unexpected change and challenges. Life changing events are about to transpire. Unconventional ideas and behavior. Also travel abroad is indicated. Impatience is heighted. Irritation with foolishness heighted. Unafraid of change.

2. Feb: Page of Swords R - Suit Tattva: Air(vayu) Card Tattva: Air of Air (vayu of vayu). Karma type: Purushartha Karma Quality: Adridha

The card is saying a lot of actions born of free will and volition will be taken but the karma quality says there won't be much lasting that comes of them.

Since it's Udana of Udana R expect lots of rumors, gossip, online trolling, conspiracy theories, accusations that in the end don't hold up, Op eds that imply things that more investigation reveals don't hold up. Upsetting news. A lot of public dramas that will stir the people up. MSM is likely to be both the conveyor and the target of this misuse of communication. In short, I would be wary of taking any news reported by any source with a grain of salt and try your own extensive fact-checking. Udana is in detriment phase for the UK this month. I would double-check even one's own posts before hitting the submit button. Sri Arya says when this card is in R anyone who speaks out risks becoming targeted by malice and malicious acts.

When Udana vayu is in detriment be wary of buying anything. By extension I would be wary of anyone trying to sell a contract. Udana R is the prana vayu of conmen, snake-oil salesmen and thieves. Sales for retail will likely seriously begin tanking and anything related to travel will be in for a world of hurt.

I'm betting by Feb UK corporations will get a good feel for how disasterous the rest of the year will be for sales. Ditto for anything travel related. Any sales gains made will be short-lived (air of air).

However, since Air of Air is in detriment I think the UK may announce a taxpayer supported boondoggle or two that will get a lot of press...and then nothing will come of it. You know how Amazon's billion dollar investment into the Rings of Power is fizzling out? Except it'd be with taxpayer money on the hook instead? Yeah...kinda like that. The boondoggle(s?) likely gain traction in fire of fire January (on paper the plan looks exciting and sound) but sold to them in Feb when the power of tricksters, conmen and thieves will be in full force.

Oh...and I would not at all be surprised if the UK starts seeing a sharp upward trend in thefts and robberies right about then too.

3. Mar: 2 of Wands R - the desire for opulence and glory but stagnation and surprise setbacks mean it will not be fulfilled. If it's not fulfilled that means it's going to convert to UK's storehouse of Agama karma. UK will experience a paralysis in will. I suspect even many ordinary people will experience this to some degree. Hanuman, sits there in the sky (like God-the-Father) looking down on the vista of glory and palatial opulence below. Hanuman, the monkey-god, is a deity of great strength (maybe he's related to Sun Wukong? I note that Sun Wukong is also a monkey deity reknown for great strength and also grand-master skill in martial arts). Hanuman is often worshiped in India by those who wish to develop talents that need great strength in all its forms in order to manifest and flourish.

However this card came up R so the UK people will be stifled in their ability to fulfill that desire so into the Agama bin it will go. Also expect a lot of spiritual flim-flam. I expect some hucksters of The Secret or New Thought genre to gain traction. Mixed in with a general stagnating environment that frustrates creative fires are unexpected and unpleasant surprises. Also indicated is that the people may be prone to relying too much on 'strong leaders' (possible rise in Ceasar-populist-wanna-be's) who will 'do something' to fulfill the desire for a return to strength, opulence and glory (Make UK Great Again?).

That opulence and glory is important. In some way this card is saying it's setting a bar that must be cleared to get something fulfilled. Since the bar is so high I expect the Truss gov will be willing to take risks that more measured PMs might not.

I'm guessing political actions might be taken that don't make much sense when a more calm, rational mindset prevails.

Weirdly this card also indicates a tendency to misuse spiritual and occult powers (that's how overwhelming some UK people's fiery, often unconscious, desires is to get fulfilled - they'll have their country pay any price to get it done). If that is true then - since it's a Fire of Fire R card - the blowback indicated will be especially deep searing and fierce even when compared to other times of occult misuse. Lets hope the UK's Magic Resistance is as inept as the U.S.'s or else it's Very interesting times for the UK coming up! :P

Sri Arya says this card holds a special meaning. It is also the card of the following -

a. entering a religious order
b. renouncing the world
c. going on a religious pilgrimage.

Expect a lot of ordinary people to cope with the upswelling chaos by turning or returning to various religions. The 2nd Religiosity is going to begin (probably quietly) trending in the UK.

comments.title: Hebrew
comments.content:
hey jmg

What do you think is the best resource for learning the esoteric meaning and symbolism of the Hebrew letters? Also, is there any lore concerning why a lot of Hebrew letters seem to be made of other letters, especially Yod?

replies.title: Re: Hebrew
replies.content: It's been a very long time since I looked into that, and I never got very deeply into it, since I'm not an Abrahamic monotheist. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Hebrew
replies.content:
Very briefly, since we're about at time for today, but a few more mundane things that might be going on with letters-within-letters:
- Some of the more simple letters are just the shapes that a pen makes that are used to make marks, kind of like how Latin letters make use of vertical lines, horizontal lines, and loops
- There's speculation that the early Phonecian alphabet which is ancestral to lots of alphabets, including the Hebrew, started out as ideograms that tried to look like the thing they represented. Kind of like how Chinese characters include "radicals" (elements that are assembled into larger graphics), some of that might have been preserved in the Hebrew one

Hope this gives you some stuff to look into!
Jeff

What is your recommendation on invoking multiple beings, rather than a single deity, at the directions? After I first learned the SOP, I only practiced it for a few months and fell off, as I continued other spiritual and energetic work, I grew into a daily practice of turning to each direction and saying:
East - "I honor and connect with my higher self"
South - "I honor and connect with my guardian angel"
West - "I honor and connect with my well-healed ancestors"
North - "I honor and connect with my physical body and it's beneficial inhabitants"
I've taken back up SOP work and recently incorporated this into the workings at each direction. Do you see problems with this, either in general, or with the correspondences?

Do I correctly recall you recommending against multiple SOPs in a day? If I live and work in multiple spaces and would like to cleanse and protect them is there a protocol for using this ritual to do so - i.e. repeat it in whole in each space, or perhaps do entire ritual for myself and then just the opening and closing for the additional spaces?

Similarly, in moments of fear or concern, say waking from a nightmare or helping an (old-enough) child who's had a nightmare, is it appropriate to do all or part of an SOP?

I worked with the Heathen LBRP and MP for many months, but though it benefited me it had adverse affects on my spouse and household and (with your advisement - thank you) I've shifted to the SOP with good effect. In those moments of "I need to banish and protect this area," the LBRP comes more readily to mind and body than the SOP, and it feels more directly effectual at this point. Is there harm in an occasional LBRP in these situations; should I stick with the SOP and trust my daily work with it to give it the same efficacy in time?

I appreciate your time and hard-won experience and understanding. Working from books has helped me; and, what has been shared in this space has enriched my understanding and my practice beyond measure. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: SOP ??
replies.content:
1) That's an interesting approach, but I know of no reason not to give it a try.

2) More than once a day regularly is probably not a good idea, but now and again is not a problem.

3) You can certainly use the LBRP at need while using the SoP for ordinary practice.

replies.title: Re: SOP ??
replies.content: Thank you.

comments.title: Solresol and occultism
comments.content:
Hey jmg

Not so much a question as a oddball observation concerning a oddball subject.

I have been reading about a failed universal language made in 1862 by a French man called Sudre. He essentially based it upon the 7 notes of the solfege scale, using the names of the notes as syllables and also invented ways for the language to not only communicated by music and speech but also colour and hand signals.

My oddball observation is that there is nothing stopping you from making the 7 notes corespondent with the 7 planetary powers via the Pythagorean system and thus using the solresol language for some kind of occultism.
Not only could the hand signals be used in a Kuji-in manner, and the language itself for chants and music, the solresol writing system joins letters together in a way similar to bind-runes and could be used for talismans and meditation themes.

https://www.omniglot.com/writing/solresol.htm

replies.title: Re: Solresol and occultism
replies.content: Hmm! One could indeed have that kind of fun with it.

replies.title: Re: Solresol and occultism
replies.content: I know, and it may be a interesting project. It certainly has a classical French enlightenment energy to it, and if the language was more successful than it was, particularly with occultists, I could imagine them studying solresol words in the same manner as cabalists study Hebrew in order to find secret meanings, and perform rituals similar to peladans and Levi’s with its symbols as a theme, or play music that is also a hidden spell to unsuspecting listeners.

replies.title: Re: Solresol and occultism
replies.content: I've downloaded some resources and scooped up some links. No clue if anything will come of it, but it strikes me in particular that it would work very well with the sevenfold structures I developed for the Dolmen Arch...

comments.title: Awakening of the old gods?
comments.content: In just the past few days I have come across a few very different sources who all make the claim that the world is the way it is because of the awakening of the old gods. One is a fringe conspiracy type and another I found was a Christian channel and author. I'm wondering if there is any basis in occult philosophy that would give validity to these sentiments? I rather think the decline of Christianity and fad witchcraft are to blame but those may themselves be symptomatic.

replies.title: Re: Awakening of the old gods?
replies.content: That's a complex matter. From an occult perspective the gods themselves never went away and never went to sleep, but their relationship to the world changes somewhat with the passing ages, and especially varies in its relation to human consciousness. We are at the end of an age of reason, and also just past the end of the Piscean age, which was ruled by Neptune and therefore had a focus on unity that tended to blur the multiplicity of the divine. So it's becoming easier for human beings to access the divine in all its complexity, and it's also becoming more common for the gods to manifest themselves to people. I expect that process to shift into overdrive in the decades ahead.

replies.title: Re: Awakening of the old gods?
replies.content: Also, we're seeing the end of the planet that rejects the divine and exalts the image of humanity standing alone in an empty universe!

replies.title: Re: Awakening of the old gods?
replies.content: Also a good point.

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection, and Nature
replies.content: If you're going to do the LBRP and MP, I recommend doing those indoors. If you want to move into something based on the SoP that's a little richer, consider my book The Druid Magic Handbook -- you don't have to do the whole thing, just take it as far as you want for now and see where it leads you.

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection, and Nature
replies.content: What's different between the LBRP and MP on the one hand and the SOP on the other that makes the later safe for doing outdoors?

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection, and Nature
replies.content: The pentagram ritual is inherently disruptive of magical energies -- that's why it's not a good idea to do it around consecrated talismans or the like -- and there are plenty of magical energies in nature, which need to flow freely without disruption. The SoP is gentler and doesn't cause that disruption.
